coredata.dc:title: Superior fluorescent ‘Color Paper-based Displayer’ for advanced anti-counterfeiting and multi-layer information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhi
Chen, Zhengjie
Qian, Jun
Ke, Xianwen
Liu, Xinghai
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent printing has attracted considerable attention due to its unique optical properties and potential applications in displays and information security. However, existing research on monochromatic printing faces challenges, particularly with respect to energy transfer, internal filter effects, and aggregation-induced quenching. To address these limitations, this study uses ethyl cellulose (EC) as a binder that forms a semi-transparent film upon drying, effectively isolating the carbon dots (CDs) and reducing detrimental energy transfer. Through systematic color tuning and performance optimization, we have developed a tri-color CD-based fluorescent ink, called ‘Superior Color Paper-based Displayer’ capable of high-fidelity color reproduction. In addition, by exploiting the excitation-dependent properties of the ink, we have designed dynamic anti-counterfeiting patterns and integrated multiple information encryption techniques. This work significantly advances the potential of full-color fluorescent printing and offers practical applications in image restoration, security enhancement, and the development of high-performance functional inks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Anti-counterfeit technology
Full-color printing
Dynamic anti-counterfeiting
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of B-CDs, G-CDs, R-CDs and fluorescent inks
Method of making three-color printing plates

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the usage of all characterizations supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2023YFE0105500), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities [Wuhan University postgraduate research credit course project of Intelligent Packaging and Food Safety (Grant No. 1506/413100017)]. The authors also thanked the Core Facility of Wuhan University for FTIR, XRD, TGA, DSC, Raman Spectra and XPS tests. Thanks to eceshi(www.eceshi.com) for the variable temperature phosphorescence test.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) B-CDs fluorescence excitation-emission spectra(Attachment: Photograph of the color of the solution at 365 nm); (b) G-CDs fluorescence excitation-emission spectra(Attachment: Photograph of the color of the solution at 365 nm); (c) R-CDs fluorescence excitation-emission spectra(Attachment: Photograph of the color of the solution at 365 nm); (d) B-ink fluorescence excitation-emission spectra; (e) G-ink fluorescence excitation-emission spectra; (f) R-ink fluorescence excitation-emission spectra; (g) FTIR spectra of B-CDs, B-ink and Blank-ink; (h) FTIR spectra of G-CDs, G-ink and Blank-ink; (i) FTIR spectra of R-CDs, R-ink and Blank-ink; (j) Transmission electron micrograph of B-CDs (Scale bar: 10 nm); (k) Histogram of the size distribution of B-CDs; (l) High-resolution transmission electron micrograph of B-CDs (Scale bar: 5 nm); (m) Transmission electron micrograph of G-CDs (Scale bar: 20 nm); (n) Histogram of the size distribution of G-CDs; (o) High-resolution transmission electron micrograph of G-CDs (Scale bar: 5 nm); (p) Transmission electron micrograph of R-CDs (Scale bar: 20 nm); (q) Histogram of the size distribution of R-CDs; (r) High-resolution transmission electron micrograph of R-CDs (Scale bar: 5 nm).

(a) B-CDs, G-CDs, R-CDs (red dotted line) and B-ink, G-ink, R-ink (blue dotted line) fluorescence colors in CIE chromaticity space; (b) UV–visible spectral absorption profiles of B-CDs, B-ink, G-CDs, G-ink, R-CDs, R-ink and Blank-ink; (c) UV–visible spectral absorption profiles of prints immersed in water solutions with different time solvents; (d) UV–visible spectral absorption profiles of prints immersed in ethanol solutions with different time solvents; (e) Percentage of fluorescent ink precipitation (in water); (f) Percentage of fluorescent ink precipitation (in ethanol); (g) Images taken under 365 nm UV-light before and after immersion in the water of the print; (h) Images taken under 365 nm UV-light before and after immersion in the ethanol of the print; (i) Rheological properties of viscosity plotted as a function of shear rate for the inks with different concentrations of EC (wt%); (j) Thixotropic properties of 8 % EC (w/v) fluorescent inks simulating screen printing process.

(a) Actual pictures of the printing effect of ink on transparent film; (b) UV light interaction with ink layers: partial absorption and transmission through stacked layers, with lower layers partially obscured; (c) Results of three-color ink overprinting tests with different stacking orders (1 = bottom, 2 = middle, 3 = top).

(a) The original design draft; (b) The flow chart of the reproduction of the design draft; (c) Actual photo of fluorescent ink reproduction; (d) Enlarged details of the print; (e) Dot details of the print.

Designed Wuhan University logo pattern, QR code, and Wuhan University architectural elements of the pattern in daylight, 254 nm, 285 nm, 310 nm, 365 nm, 395 nm, and 410 nm of the actual picture, respectively.

(a) Actual picture of the designed Morse code encryption at 365 nm, as well as the decryption process and the information obtained from the decryption (WU HAN UNIVERSITY NO 1); (b) Actual picture of the designed Morse code encryption at 285 nm, as well as the decryption process and the information obtained from the decryption (WU HAN IS NO 1); (c) Actual picture of the designed Morse code encryption at 410 nm, as well as the decryption process and the information obtained from the decryption (W A N).
Superior Color 'Paper-based Displayer' Production Process and Application Chart.

head.ce:title: Superior fluorescent ‘Color Paper-based Displayer’ for advanced anti-counterfeiting and multi-layer information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Engineering Technology Research Center of Spectrum and Imaging Instrument, Electronic Information School, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Engineering Technology Research Center of Spectrum and Imaging Instrument

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Engineering Technology Research Center of Spectrum and Imaging Instrument, Electronic Information School, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nursing, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Nursing, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuhan Institute of Quantum Technology, Wuhan 430206, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wuhan Institute of Quantum Technology, Wuhan 430206, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Hubei Engineering Technology Research Center of Spectrum and Imaging Instrument, Electronic Information School, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Engineering Technology Research Center of Spectrum and Imaging Instrument

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent printing has attracted considerable attention due to its unique optical properties and potential applications in displays and information security. However, existing research on monochromatic printing faces challenges, particularly with respect to energy transfer, internal filter effects, and aggregation-induced quenching. To address these limitations, this study uses ethyl cellulose (EC) as a binder that forms a semi-transparent film upon drying, effectively isolating the carbon dots (CDs) and reducing detrimental energy transfer. Through systematic color tuning and performance optimization, we have developed a tri-color CD-based fluorescent ink, called ‘Superior Color Paper-based Displayer’ capable of high-fidelity color reproduction. In addition, by exploiting the excitation-dependent properties of the ink, we have designed dynamic anti-counterfeiting patterns and integrated multiple information encryption techniques. This work significantly advances the potential of full-color fluorescent printing and offers practical applications in image restoration, security enhancement, and the development of high-performance functional inks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, CDs have garnered widespread attention [1,2] as an emerging class of fluorescent nanomaterials due to their unique physicochemical properties [3,4]. These nanoparticles not only exhibit excellent water solubility and low toxicity but also demonstrate favorable biocompatibility [5], making them promising candidates for applications in biomedical imaging [6], sensors [7], and optoelectronic devices [8]. The tunable photoluminescent (PL) characteristics of CDs reveal significant potential in image reconstruction and display technologies, particularly in dynamic displays and information encryption [9,10,11,12].

With advancements in printing technology, fluorescent ink printing has gradually emerged as an innovative technique with significant potential in the fields of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption [13,14]. Fluorescent inks based on fluorescent materials such as CDs can emit bright colors under excitation by specific wavelengths of light, displaying unique dynamic photoresponsive properties [15,16,17]. Existing studies have thoroughly validated the application prospects of these materials. For example, Wang et al. [18] successfully synthesized full-color CDs capable of emitting red, green, and blue fluorescence by adjusting the protonation of solvents using water, glycerol, and Dimethylformamide (DMF) in a one-step solvothermal process. These CDs exhibit excellent dispersibility in water and enhanced stability through hydrazine passivation, laying a solid foundation for the long-term application of fluorescent inks. In the preparation of fluorescent inks, cellulose, as the most abundant natural polymer, serves as an ideal binder due to its high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excellent film-forming properties [19,20]. Chen et al. [21] developed a cellulose-based flexible transparent film that exhibits prominent blue fluorescence under UV light, showing significant potential in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. This cellulose-based material is cost-effective, suitable for large-scale production, and has promising applications in smart security packaging and labeling, making it an ideal material for fluorescent anti-counterfeiting.

Another important innovation in fluorescent inks is photoresponsive pattern technology, which allows switching between colorless and luminescent full-color states and is regarded as a breakthrough for high-security anti-counterfeiting [22,23]. He et al. [24] synthesized hydrophobic CDs with red emission using perylene dianhydride and aromatic amine precursors. Through surface functionalization, these CDs achieved efficient solid-state red-light emission and strong fluorescence intensity [25]. Fluorescent ink, prepared with polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as a binder, was applied in information anti-counterfeiting, offering the dual advantages of low cost and encryption capability, demonstrating potential in commercial anti-counterfeiting ink applications.

Despite significant progress in CD synthesis methods and performance optimization, challenges remain in their application for multicolor printing [26]. The fluorescence performance of CDs is often weakened due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [27,28] between different CDs, internal filtering effects (IFE) [29], and aggregation-induced quenching [30], resulting in reduced emission intensity and uneven coloration. These limitations not only restrict the potential of CDs in multicolor printing and display technologies but also pose challenges for their further application in advanced security and information encryption [31,32]. Therefore, overcoming these technical obstacles will be crucial to enhancing the performance of CDs in complex information encryption and dynamic anti-counterfeiting applications. However, most traditional optical materials rely on static properties or single signals, which limits their application potential in advanced anti-counterfeiting and information encryption [33]. In contrast, fluorescent ink materials with dynamic photoresponsive properties offer unprecedented opportunities for advanced anti-counterfeiting [34], demonstrating greater application potential and significant competitive advantages.

To address the technical challenges of multilayered anti-counterfeiting in printing, this study focuses on the development and application of red, green, and blue CDs. By synthesizing excitation-dependent CDs and systematically investigating their physical and optical properties, we reveal their tunable color variations under different excitation wavelengths, providing a solid foundation for multifunctional printing applications Scheme 1 .

Building on this, we have successfully combined the three CDs with EC to formulate a screen-printable fluorescent ink. The semi-transparency of EC after drying not only effectively isolates fluorescence interference between the CDs, but also greatly enhances color independence, ensuring the purity and stability of printed patterns. In addition, by precisely controlling the ink’s viscosity, drying time and adhesion to paper substrates, we achieved accurate and vivid color reproduction.

Using the prepared fluorescent ink, we reproduced colorful patterns on paper, achieving an advanced dynamic anti-counterfeiting effect. Utilizing the unique responsiveness of CDs under different excitation wavelengths, the designed patterns exhibited distinct color shifts under specific light sources, providing an innovative visual anti-counterfeiting solution. In addition, the tunable color properties of CDs were exploited to develop a novel information encryption method that utilizes color differences under multi-wavelength excitation to convey layered information and enable complex and efficient encryption.

This study integrates the dynamic optical properties of CDs with EC to develop a novel screen-printable fluorescent ink. By synthesizing excitation-dependent red, green, and blue CDs and addressing fluorescence interference and aggregation-induced quenching, we achieved superior color stability and environmental resistance. The ink’s tunable fluorescence and multi-wavelength excitation properties enable high-fidelity color reproduction, dynamic anti-counterfeiting designs, and innovative encryption methods. These advances establish a new paradigm for secure, multifunctional printing technologies with significant potential for advanced anti-counterfeiting and information security applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of B-CDs, G-CDs, R-CDs and fluorescent inks
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence excitation/emission spectroscopy was used to determine the optimal excitation wavelengths and emission peaks of three types of CDs (B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs) and their corresponding inks to assess the effect of EC on their luminescence properties. The results are illustrated in Fig. 1
(a), (b), and (c). The optimal excitation wavelengths for B-CDs were determined to be 310–320 nm and 380 nm, with an emission peak at 445 nm. The quantum yield (QY) of B-CDs was measured to be 72.8 %. For G-CDs, the optimal excitation wavelengths are 250 nm, 270–290 nm, and 400 nm, with an emission peak at 515 nm. The QY of G-CDs was measured to be 70.2 %. In the case of R-CDs, the optimal excitation wavelengths are 380–390 nm and 280 nm, also with an emission peak at 515 nm. The QY of R-CDs was measured to be 12.6 %. After dispersing the CDs into the EC ink, as shown in Fig. 1 (d), (e), and (f), and the pictures of CDs solution and ink under daylight and UV light are shown in Fig. S1, it is observed that, with the exception of the R-ink, whose optimal excitation wavelength changed, the remaining CDs exhibited no discernible influence from EC. Their optimal excitation wavelengths and emission peaks remained unaltered, suggesting that EC is a suitable connecting material for fluorescent inks, demonstrating no impact on fluorescence and maintaining relatively high fluorescence emission peaks. Notably, the addition of EC resulted in a shift in the optimal excitation wavelengths of the R-CDs from 380-390 nm and 280 nm to 370–380 nm and 290–300 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission, originally observed near 380 nm, shifted to a wavelength near 300 nm. This shift in emission wavelength presents a potential avenue for subsequent anti-counterfeiting applications and information encryption. The data indicate that the three inks possess distinct optimal excitation wavelengths, which have informed the development of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption programs based on this characteristic. The complexity of CDs luminescence mechanisms is well-established, with factors like size, surface defects, and functionalization all playing a role in modulating emission. For the R-CDs in our study, their unique photoluminescence behavior is likely due to the interplay between their size and surface chemistry [35,36].

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to investigate the chemical structure and functional groups of EC and the three fluorescent inks, with the goal of identifying key absorption peaks and understanding the interaction between EC and CDs, the results reveal characteristic absorption bands for each component. The infrared absorption peak of EC ink (blank-ink) is illustrated in Fig. 1 (g), (h), (i), where a broad absorption peak appears at 3500 cm−1 and between 3000 cm−1 and 2800 cm−1, corresponding to O-H and C-H stretching vibrations in EC [37]. Additionally, the peak at 1120 cm−1 arises from the vibration of the C-O-C symmetry ring [38]. These absorption peaks are also evident in the infrared absorption spectra of the three fluorescent inks. The B-CDs demonstrate absorption peaks in the range of 1590–1630 cm−1, which are attributed to amide II CN stretching vibrations and NH in-plane deformation vibrations [39]. Furthermore, the G-CDs display distinctive bands associated with strong O-H, CC, and C-O stretching vibrations at 3440 cm−1, 1630 cm−1, and 1100 cm−1 [40], respectively. The R-CDs also show absorption peaks near 1590 cm−1, similarly attributed to amide II CN stretching vibrations and NH in-plane deformation vibrations [39]. However, these absorption peaks, along with the characteristic peaks of EC, are also present in the infrared spectra of B-ink and G-ink, with no novel characteristic peaks observed. In contrast, R-ink exhibits a novel and robust absorption peak at 950 cm−1, which is attributed to the C-O-C antisymmetric stretching vibration. This may explain the shift in the optimal emission peak of the R-CDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were used to study the morphology, structure and lattice spacing of the CDs. Particle size distributions were analysed using ImageJ software to assess the size and dispersion of the CDs, providing insight into the correlation between size and optical properties. Fig. 1 (j), (k), (l) illustrates the morphological and size characterization of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy observations indicate that all three types of CDs exhibit a spherical structure and are highly dispersed. Furthermore, the lattice spacing of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs was determined to be 0.21 nm, 0.21 nm, and 0.23 nm (Fig. 1 (m), (n), (o)), respectively, as assessed by high-resolution transmission electron micrographs (HRTEM), corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite [41]. To further analyze the size and distribution of the three types of CDs, the particle size was evaluated using Image J software, resulting in histograms of their particle size distribution (see Fig. 1 (p), (q), (r)). As illustrated in the figure, the particle size distribution range of B-CDs is between 2.1 and 3.3 nm, with an average particle size of approximately 2.84 nm. The particle size distribution range of G-CDs is between 2.8 and 5.2 nm, with an average particle size of approximately 4.14 nm. The particle size distribution range of R-CDs is between 4.8 and 8.0 nm, with an average particle size of approximately 6.39 nm. The R-CDs, being the largest in size, exhibit distinct behavior due to their larger surface area, which facilitates stronger interactions with EC, leading to altered photoluminescence properties. This may also be responsible for the change in its excitation wavelength [42]. As the particle size of the CDs increases, their emission wavelengths also increase, resulting in a red shift of their colors. This observation is consistent with the size effect of carbon quantum dots and aligns with findings reported in the existing literature [43].

The Commission Internationale de ĺEclairage (CIE) was used to evaluate the fluorescence color changes of the CDs and inks, highlighting the effect of EC on the R-CDs. UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured to assess the absorption characteristics of the CDs and inks and to investigate the spectral shifts caused by the incorporation of EC. Fig. 2
(a) illustrates the CIE coordinates corresponding to the fluorescence of the three luminescent CDs and the three inks at 365 nm, designated as BGR, B-ink, G-ink, and R-ink, respectively. It is evident that the EC binder significantly influences the color of R-CDs. According to the principles of color and light addition [44], the colors represented in the triangle can be theoretically realized. Fig. S2 shows the possible colors that can be rendered by the three-color ink we designed. Fig. 2 (b) depicts the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, alongside red, green, and blue tricolor inks, as well as pure EC inks. As shown in Fig. 2 (b), B-CDs and B-inks exhibit pronounced absorption peaks at approximately 320 nm and 375 nm, respectively. Notably, these peaks remain largely unchanged upon the addition of ink, indicating a high degree of stability. G-CDs and G-inks display similar characteristics, with absorption peaks observed at 252 nm, 290 nm, and around 330 nm. The aforementioned absorption peaks and optimal excitation wavelengths align closely with the fluorescence emission spectra presented in the preceding article. R-CDs exhibit distinct absorption peaks at 248 nm and 285 nm; however, upon the addition of EC, these peaks shift to 250 nm and 300 nm, respectively. This suggests that the incorporation of EC selectively influences the R-CDs, potentially due to the formation of a novel functional group, which may result in a shift in the position of the absorption peaks.

To assess the water resistance of the printed ink, a dip test was carried out in both water and ethanol solutions, followed by a UV absorption test to monitor the release of ink from the paper. This approach allows the stability of the inks to be assessed in different solvent environments, providing insight into the impact of these solutions on the durability and resistance of the ink. A small sample of non-fluorescent paper printed with three colors of ink was submerged in 15 mL of both water and ethanol, ensuring complete immersion in each solution. The solutions underwent UV absorption testing at specified time intervals, with the results illustrated in Fig. 2 (c) and (d). As the duration of submergence increased, the UV absorption in both the water and ethanol solutions also rose. Given that all three inks exhibit absorption peaks near 280 nm, this wavelength was designated as a detection benchmark. After 60 min of submergence in the water solution, the UV absorption value was recorded at less than 0.02. In contrast, the ethanol solution exhibited an absorption value of approximately 0.07. The absorption value at 280 nm for the ethanol solution is considered as a reference over the 60-minute period, during which the release of ink from both aqueous and ethanol solutions was monitored at various intervals. The findings are presented in Fig. 2 (e) and (f). The printed material reached its maximum absorption value in the aqueous solution after a one-minute soak, approximately 18 %, and maintained this peak for the subsequent 60 min, indicating minimal ink release in water. Conversely, in the ethanol solution, the ink release increased with time, achieving 90 % release within approximately 15 min and reaching complete saturation at 100 % after 30 min. This suggests that the ink is readily released in an ethanol environment. This behavior can be attributed to the inherent properties of EC, a water-soluble cellulose derivative, which demonstrates excellent moisture resistance. Fig. 2 (g) and (h) displays the results of soaking the stone before and after its pattern under ultraviolet light, further confirming that the stone exhibits good water resistance in aqueous solutions, while the pattern is largely obliterated in alcohol solutions.

The water contact angle test is an effective method for evaluating the surface properties of a material. The hydrophobicity of the material surface can be determined by measuring the water contact angle [45]. Consequently, the contact angle test was conducted on the surface of the printed materials, and the results are presented in Fig. S3. As illustrated in the figure, the average contact angle of the printed ink on the surface was 73.2° after the initial 0-second measurement. Following a continuous 30 s test, the contact angle exhibited minimal change and ultimately remained in the 73.3°. The paper surface without printing ink exhibited an average contact angle of approximately 72.2° in the initial 0-second measurement. Following a continuous 30-second test, the contact angle demonstrated a notable decrease, reaching approximately 14.3° after 30 s. This phenomenon can be attributed to the capillary action of the paper fibers, which absorbed water from the surface. Therefore, it can be demonstrated that the ink exhibits excellent water resistance following printing on the paper surface. The paper fibres are unable to absorb water, which provides further evidence of the hydrophobic nature of the ink and confirms its ability to safeguard the colour of printed matter effectively in high humidity environments.

Storage and UV exposure tests were carried out to assess the stability and durability of the printed materials. The storage test assesses the fluorescence retention over one month at room temperature, while the UV exposure test examines the effect of prolonged irradiation on fluorescence intensity and colour stability. A rub test was also carried out to measure the print's resistance to mechanical wear. The storage time of the printed materials is also a relevant factor to consider. The printed materials will be exposed to the air, stored at room temperature for 1 month, and will not undergo shading treatment. During the daytime, the materials will be irradiated with indoor white light every 5 d with 365 nm UV-light to capture their fluorescence image. The results are presented in Fig. S4. It can be observed that, over the course of 30 d, the fluorescence color is not discernible to the naked eye. This indicates that the material can be preserved in a typical environment for at least one month without significant alteration. Furthermore, the prints were subjected to 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation for 10 h, with a 1-hour interval, to capture their fluorescence pattern. The results are shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence color did not change much under up to 8 h of irradiation, and only the fluorescence intensity was slightly reduced, and the color was faintly hairy. At the same time, we tested its stability in extreme environments. It was placed in a humid environment with R = 75 % for 12 d, and its fluorescence image was taken every four days, as shown in Fig. S6(a). It was placed in a high temperature environment with 60 °C for 12 d, and its fluorescence image was taken every four days, as shown in Fig. S6(b). It can maintain good stability in these extreme environments, and its fluorescence color does not change with the naked eye. It shows that it has low environmental requirements and can adapt to most of the daily scenes. This revealed that the CDs are susceptible to oxidation and deterioration in the presence of air, resulting in a notable fluorescence decline. However, the EC filler serves to a certain extent as an isolating agent for the CDs, preventing contact with oxygen. Following the printing of the outermost layer of B-CDs after the hydrazine hydrate passivation, the stability of the CDs is significantly enhanced, which may be the case. This may also improve the overall longevity of the fluorescent ink, particularly in maintaining its fluorescent color stability in printed materials over time, due to the protective film-forming properties of EC. Subsequently, the quality of the prints was evaluated, and they were subjected to a friction test in accordance with the Chinese standard GB/T 7706–2008. A hard plastic body with a surface roughness of 1.60 μm was utilized, and the friction speed for the friction test was 45 times/min, with a stroke of approximately 60 mm. The color of the printed matter was examined after 50, 200, 500, 1000, and 2000 times of friction, respectively, in accordance with this standard. The color difference between the obtained color and the color before friction is illustrated in Fig. S7. The fluorescence images after different times of rubbing are shown in Fig. S8. The results demonstrate that this print exhibits superior resistance to friction. Even after 2000 cycles of friction, the color difference remains within 1, and the fluorescence image is still sharp, indicating the print’s durability and resilience.

The steady-state viscosity test evaluates the ink’s flow behaviour at different shear rates, while the thixotropic behaviour test simulates the ink’s performance at different stages of the screen printing process, assessing its ability to recover viscosity after set-off. In the context of screen printing, the viscosity of the ink in question represents a crucial indicator [46]. In general, the viscosity of screen printing ink is optimal within the range of 4000 to 12000 mPa·s. An excessively viscous ink is not conducive to the transfer of ink, while an ink of insufficient viscosity will leak too much ink, which will result in the formation of unclear patterns in the printed matter. Fig. 2 (i) depicts the experimental outcomes pertaining to steady-state viscosity at varying shear rates (0.1–1000 s−1). As the shear rate increases, the inks of varying concentrations exhibit pronounced shear thinning behavior, characteristic of pseudoplastic fluids. In order to achieve optimal printing quality, the ink concentration range of 4000–12000 mPa·s at 100 s−1 was selected as the final experimental group [47]. This resulted in the conclusion that 8 % (w/v) ethylcellulose is the optimal linker. The distinctive characteristics of the screen printing process dictate that inks utilized in this method should possess particular rheological attributes. Prior to full-scale printing, inks must be loaded onto the machine with a high viscosity at low shear rates to ensure stability. However, during the screen printing process, the shear force temporarily requires a low viscosity, and the printed ink should subsequently return to a high viscosity state [48]. Fig. 2 (j) illustrates the thixotropic behavior of the inks, as determined through experimentation in three successive stages with varying shear rates. Each stage corresponds to a distinct screen printing process. In the initial stage, a shear rate of 0.1 s−1 was maintained for 30 s to simulate the loading of fluorescent ink onto the screen. Subsequently, a sudden increase in shear rate to 300 s−1 was applied to simulate the printing speed during screen printing, thereby exerting pressure on the ink flow through the screen. Subsequently, as the ink was deposited onto the substrate, the shear rate was drastically reduced to 0.1 s−1, and this shear rate was maintained for 180 s to restore ink elasticity. Dynamic viscosity plots demonstrated that the fluorescent ink displayed notable thixotropic behavior as the three stages advanced. The viscosity recovery was found to be greater than 84 %. This value was found to be 71 %, thereby confirming that the ink displays high elasticity and is suitable for forming fine patterns with increased fidelity [49,50]. At the same time, the drying speed of this ink is also very fast, after printing in 30 s the ink on the surface can be completely dry (See Supporting Video 1). Therefore, this ink can be well adapted to the requirements of screen printing, and the prints have good environmental adaptability, waterproof, friction resistance, and can be stored for a long time. It is an ink that can be used as an excellent anti-counterfeiting and information encryption printing.

The translucent state of the EC ink after drying (Fig. 3
a) indicates that the order of ink overprinting significantly influences the final color effect, and the light transmittance of the printed film was tested by UV–visible spectroscopy, and its transmittance was 43 %. UV light passing through each layer of ink is partially absorbed, while the ink in the lowest layer is partially obscured by the upper layer (Fig. 3 b). To restore the original color of the image, a three-color ink overprinting test was conducted using various sequences (Fig. 3 c, the number represents the order of stacking 1 is stacked at the bottom, 3 is stacked at the top). The position of the colors of its individual parts in CIE chromaticity space is shown in Fig. S9. The results demonstrated that the optimal overprinting order for green, red, and blue (No.3) yielded the most favorable color restoration. A comparison of No.3 and No.5 reveals that red ink exhibits a bright yellow color when overprinted on green ink, but not on blue ink. This finding indicates that the red fluorescence is stronger than the blue fluorescence when red ink is overprinted on green ink. Furthermore, the combination of No.3 and No.1 shows that a bright cyan color appears only when blue ink is overprinted on top of green ink. According to the naked eye observation and its relative position in CIE chromaticity space, it can be concluded that the optimal overprinting sequence is green, red, and blue. Consequently, all subsequent color images will be printed following this sequence. The light transmittance of the printed film was tested by UV–visible spectroscopy, and its transmittance was 43 %.

Fig. 4
(a) illustrates a full-color pattern that incorporates elements of Wuhan University. This pattern was divided into colors and subsequently restored after overprinting. The specific process employed is depicted in schematic Fig. 4 (b), which was designed and separated into RGB color channels using Photoshop. Printing plates were then created for each color, and the pattern shown in Fig. 4 (c) was formed by overlaying the optimal three-color fluorescent ink, prepared as previously described, onto non-fluorescent paper in the sequence of green, red, and blue through screen printing, and it is imperative that the ink from the preceding print run be permitted to dry thoroughly before commencing the next print run. The particular printing process and resulting product are illustrated in Supporting Video 2. By utilizing screen printing color separation technology and integrating the red, green, and blue layers of fluorescent ink, the image effect reliant on fluorescence excitation has been successfully achieved. The fluorescent ink exhibits dynamic anti-counterfeiting properties, enabling color shifts in response to varying light sources. The film-forming properties of EC are employed throughout the printing process. Due to its non-fluorescent characteristics, EC can rapidly form a stable film, effectively maintaining the color rendering and stability of the fluorescent ink. In natural daylight, the printed image appears colorless; however, when exposed to 365 nm ultraviolet light, the pattern vividly showcases the colorful representation of Wuhan University. Fig. 4 (d) presents a magnified representation of the print, clearly illustrating the moiré patterns produced by the multi-color overprinting process. The red, green, and blue light-emitting dots are distinctly arranged, adhering to the principles of additive and chromatic light, which electronic screens also follow to create the perception of continuous color patterns. This visual effect is referred to as an advanced color ‘paper display’ The advancement of this technology not only exemplifies the potential of fluorescent ink for high-precision image reproduction but also paves the way for future developments in fluorescent dynamic anti-counterfeiting and information encryption technologies on paper materials. Its excellent color and light reproduction can display colorful patterns, which can provide more wonderful patterns for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications. Our advantage over other jobs is that full color reproduction comparable to screens, advanced printing quality and stability, specific comparisons are listed in Table S1.

A variety of patterns were developed and printed for use in anti-counterfeiting applications that require a high degree of flexibility and responsiveness. As shown in Fig. 5 , we employed dynamic fluorescent transparent inks to produce three distinct patterns: the Wuhan University logo, a QR code, and an architectural element. These patterns are nearly invisible in daylight but exhibit unique dynamic anti-counterfeiting properties under ultraviolet excitation at different wavelengths. Under 254 nm UV light, the Wuhan University logo, QR code, and architectural pattern all display a prominent green fluorescence effect. At 285 nm and 310 nm excitation, the red fluorescence within the patterns becomes more pronounced, adding a richer color layering effect to the overall image. When exposed to 365 nm UV light, the printed patterns closely resemble the original colored designs. Under 395 nm excitation, the blue and green fluorescence intensifies, while the red fluorescence diminishes significantly, resulting in a cyan visual effect. Finally, at 410 nm excitation, the red fluorescence completely disappears, leaving only blue and green fluorescence, which creates a cool-toned visual effect. This multi-wavelength fluorescence response not only highlights the potential of these inks in high-precision dynamic image reproduction but also provides innovative technical support and diverse visual expression for fluorescent anti-counterfeiting design.

Inspired by the unique optical properties of fluorescent inks, we have developed an advanced information encryption scheme based on Morse code to achieve multi-layered security. As illustrated in Fig. 6
(a), under 365 nm excitation, the encrypted information is fully displayed, consisting of letters and numbers that read: WUHAN UNIVERSITY NO 1. The excitation-dependent characteristics of the fluorescent inks provide multiple layers of encryption. When the excitation source is switched to 285 nm (Fig. 6 (b)), the visible information changes to: WUHAN IS NO 1, resulting in incomplete information. This alteration occurs because the blue fluorescence is significantly weakened under 285 nm excitation, rendering it nearly invisible, as can be clearly observed in the image. Furthermore, under 410 nm excitation (Fig. 6 (c)), only the green fluorescent components are visible, revealing the content: W A N (an abbreviation for 'I love you' in Chinese), thus forming a third level of encrypted information. The three fluorescent inks we developed each exhibit optimal emission under different excitation wavelengths, demonstrating superior anti-counterfeiting performance compared to traditional fluorescent materials. This multi-wavelength responsive encryption technology presents new possibilities for information security and anti-counterfeiting design, providing an advantage that is difficult to replicate.

This study illustrates the potential of CDs for a diverse array of applications in fluorescent inks and printing technologies. The core objective of this research is to optimize the color tuning and performance of CDs to overcome the challenges in multi-color printing, enhancing dynamic anti-counterfeiting effects and enabling multi- layer information encryption. By optimizing the color tuning and performance of CDs, we have successfully addressed the key challenges in multi-color printing, resulting in enhanced color performance and dynamic anti-counterfeiting effects. The application of EC effectively isolates the energy transfer between different colors of CDs, ensuring the clarity and stability of the printed pattern. The degree of color reproduction is comparable to that of an electronic screen on paper. Additionally, the printed matter is water-resistant and friction-resistant. The printed matter is waterproof, friction-proof, long-lasting, and exhibits other beneficial characteristics. It is suitable for use as a carrier of anti-counterfeiting technology and information encryption technology. The material exhibits a wide range of color changes when exposed to different UV light wavelengths (254–410 nm), which significantly enhances its anti-counterfeiting properties. Its excitation-dependent characteristics also make it suitable for use as a carrier in information encryption technology, with the potential for multi-stage encryption. The data suggest that this study presents a novel fluorescent printing strategy with significant potential for integration into modern printing and information security technologies. Further research may elucidate its potential applications in other high-end fields and facilitate its broader implementation across a range of disciplines.

All chemicals from commercial sources are of analytical grade. Anhydrous ethanol, anhydrous methanol, ammonias solution were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethyl cellulose (ethoxyl content 48 %, 200 mPa/s), phloroglucinol anhydrous, p-phenylenediamine, hydrazine monohydrate were from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Naphthalene diamine was from Anhui Zesheng Technology Co., Ltd. (Anhui, China). Citric acid was from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The synthesis of B-CDs was obtained by slightly improving the method with reference to the previous method [51]. The specific synthesis method is: Citric acid (300 mg) and diaminonaphthalene (50 mg) in ethanol (40 mL) was prepared. Following a 10 min sonication period, the transparent precursor solution was transferred to an autoclave (100 mL) lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. At the conclusion of the reaction, the system was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. Subsequently, 6 mL of ammonia and 0.02 mL of hydrazine hydrate were added to the obtained carbon point solution for surface passivation, and the mixture was allowed to react at 200 °C for 1 h. At the conclusion of the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature naturally. A dichloromethane-anhydrous methanol mixture was employed as the eluent, with an eluent ratio of 4:1 to 2:1. This process was repeated several times to ensure the isolation of pure B-CDs.

Homotrienol PG (2000 mg) was dissolved in ethanol (40 mL) in accordance with the standard protocol. Following an ultrasonic dissolution period of 10 min, the transparent precursor solution was transferred to an autoclave (100 mL) lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. At the conclusion of the reaction, the solution was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. G-CDs were purified by silica gel column chromatography using a mixture of dichloromethane and anhydrous methanol as the eluent. During the purification process, the polarity of the eluent was altered dynamically by modifying the volume ratio of dichloromethane to methanol. In particular, the volume ratio was maintained within the range of 200:1 to 150:1 throughout the purification process. This process was repeated several times to ensure the isolation of pure G-CDs.

A solution of 40 mg of p-phenylenediamine and 40 mL of ethanol was prepared by sonication for 10 min. This clear precursor solution was then transferred to an autoclave (100 mL) lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. Once the reaction was complete, the mixture was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The eluent, a mixture of dichloromethane and anhydrous methanol, was used in a ratio of 25:1 to 15:1. This process was repeated several times to ensure the isolation of pure R-CDs.

Anhydrous ethanol is used at a specified concentration in a 15 mL carbon point solution, which is then subjected to magnetic stirring. Subsequently, 1.5 g of ethylcellulose is added slowly and repeatedly. The ink was prepared by dissolving 10 % (V/W) ethylcellulose in water, stirring the solution at room temperature for 24 h, and then subjecting it to ultrasonic treatment for 30 min to remove residual gases. This process resulted in the production of a uniform and transparent fluorescent ink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method of making three-color printing plates
ce:section.ce:para: The objective is to create a full-colour pattern in Photoshop for colour separation, resulting in the generation of a monochrome pattern comprising R, G, and B channels. This monochrome pattern will then undergo automatic screening in the software, utilising a half-hanging screen and a screening frequency of 50 lines per inch. The screening angle will be set at 15°, 45°, and 75°, respectively, with a screen mesh of 200 mesh. The final three-colour stencil is thus obtained. The substrate used for printing is non-fluorescent A4-sized paper. And the printing is done with a 20 cm square screen printing scraper to achieve consistent and uniform coverage.

Zhi Li: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Conceptualization. Zhengjie Chen: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jun Qian: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Xianwen Ke: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xinghai Liu: Software, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the usage of all characterizations supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2023YFE0105500), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities [Wuhan University postgraduate research credit course project of Intelligent Packaging and Food Safety (Grant No. 1506/413100017)]. The authors also thanked the Core Facility of Wuhan University for FTIR, XRD, TGA, DSC, Raman Spectra and XPS tests. Thanks to eceshi(www.eceshi.com) for the variable temperature phosphorescence test.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.160047.

ce:section.ce:para:
